summary:
This bill, titled the Kids Online Safety and Privacy Act, primarily aims to establish new protections for minors on the internet by imposing requirements on certain online platforms and updating children’s privacy law, while also containing a separate title to reduce duplicative government reports.

Title I creates a “duty of care” for “covered platforms” (broadly defined as online platforms, video games, messaging apps, and video streaming services likely used by minors, with numerous exceptions for entities like internet service providers, email services, schools, and libraries). These platforms must exercise reasonable care in designing features to prevent and mitigate specific harms to minors, such as anxiety, depression, eating disorders, bullying, and sexual exploitation. The bill mandates that platforms provide minors with easy-to-use safeguards, set to the most protective levels by default, to limit unwanted communications, control personalized recommendation systems, restrict geolocation sharing, and manage time spent on the platform. Parental tools for managing privacy and account settings are also required, enabled by default for users under 13. Covered platforms must publish annual transparency reports based on independent third-party audits assessing risks of harm to minors. The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and state attorneys general are given authority to enforce the act, which generally takes effect 18 months after enactment. The title also requires large online platforms that use opaque, user-data-driven algorithms to give users the option to switch to an “input-transparent algorithm” that primarily relies on information the user expressly provides. State laws are only preempted if they directly conflict with this title, and states may enact stronger protections.

Title II amends the Children’s Online Privacy Protection Act of 1998 (COPPA) to extend its protections to teens aged 13 to 16, in addition to children under 13. It expands the definition of personal information to include items like photographs, voice recordings, and geolocation information. The bill prohibits operators from collecting, using, or disclosing a child or teen’s personal information for targeted advertising and requires that data not be retained longer than necessary. It establishes rights for parents and teens to access, correct, and delete personal information, and prohibits an operator from terminating service because a deletion request is made.

Title III requires federal agencies to include in their annual budget justifications a list of recurring reports to Congress, identify any that are outdated or duplicative, and provide a recommendation to sunset, modify, or consolidate them.
